items.q: What is overextension in child language?
items.correct: When a child applies a word more broadly than adults do — e.g. calling all four-legged animals 'dog' before learning distinct categories.
items.misconception: Overextension is when children use very long words they don't understand.
items.random_wrong: Overextension is when children speak too loudly.
items.why: Overextension is about semantic categories, not word length or volume. It shows children are forming hypotheses about meaning, then refining them — a key window into acquisition.

items.q: What is the difference between semantics and pragmatics?
items.correct: Semantics studies literal meaning of words and sentences; pragmatics studies how meaning depends on context, speaker intention and conversational implicature.
items.misconception: Semantics is grammar and pragmatics is vocabulary.
items.random_wrong: Semantics is speech and pragmatics is writing.
items.why: 'Can you pass the salt?' semantically asks ability; pragmatically requests salt. Students miss that pragmatics governs the gap between literal meaning and intended meaning.

items.q: Are claims that women use more 'tentative' language well-supported?
items.correct: Early studies (Lakoff) made broad claims, but later research (e.g. Holmes, Cameron) shows context matters more than gender, and many supposed differences disappear with proper data.
items.misconception: Women always use more hedges and tag questions than men.
items.random_wrong: Women and men have different vocal cords so speak differently.
items.why: Stereotypes about gendered language are widely repeated in textbooks but contested by current research. Students inherit Lakoff's claims without the critique that has followed.
Correct answer: Every sentence and proper nouns. “Only proper nouns” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
Exact answer: 3. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
